items.q: Why do tertiary halogenoalkanes typically react via SN1 rather than SN2?
items.correct: The tertiary carbocation intermediate is stabilised by three alkyl groups (positive inductive effect), making the SN1 pathway favourable.
items.misconception: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN1 because they are more reactive in every way.
items.random_wrong: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN2 because they have more bonds to break.
items.why: Students mix up steric and electronic factors — they don't realise it's the STABILITY of the carbocation (electronic) plus STERIC HINDRANCE (blocking SN2) that drives the SN1 preference.

items.q: What does a curly arrow in an organic mechanism represent?
items.correct: The movement of a pair of electrons from where they are to where they end up.
items.misconception: A curly arrow shows the movement of an atom from one position to another.
items.random_wrong: A curly arrow shows the direction in which heat is flowing in the reaction.
items.why: Curly arrows look like they're pointing to atoms, so students think they describe atomic motion rather than electron pair movement.

items.q: Can you determine the order of a reaction with respect to a reactant from the balanced equation?
items.correct: No — orders must be determined experimentally; they often differ from the stoichiometric coefficients.
items.misconception: The order of reaction with respect to each reactant equals its coefficient in the balanced equation.
items.random_wrong: The order of reaction is always equal to the number of reactants in the equation.
items.why: Students see numbers in the balanced equation and try to reuse them — but rate laws depend on the mechanism, not just stoichiometry.
It's Trigonal bipyramidal-like, 5 partial bonds. “Tetrahedral cation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: 3° R-X. “1° R-X” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
